study_items.title: Syria
study_items.original_text:
It is hard to tell exactly where the noise is coming
from, but impossible to miss it from anywhere
in Damascus: all day and night you can hear
the dull thud and boom of artillery, rockets or
planes pounding rebel positions – the sound of
war getting closer to Syria’s capital. But just over
two years into the Syrian crisis – the longest
and bloodiest of the Arab uprisings – ignoring
the sound of death and destruction nearby has
become the new normal for Damascenes.

Over the weekend, men could be seen puffing
on water pipes in a palm-shaded park, children
playing between the flowerbeds and couples
chatting on benches as the unmistakable
thunderclap of high explosive could be heard
a few miles away – smoke rising between the
minarets of a nearby Ottoman-era mosque. No
one seemed to notice.

“Actually you do get used to it after a while,” said
George, an IT technician from a village on the
coast. “But you never know exactly what they are
hitting.” That usually becomes clear later from
video clips posted by opposition media outlets
on YouTube.

The sinister background noise is doubly
disturbing because the government tries so hard
to preserve a jaunty air of business as usual. “As
you can see, everything here is fine but we have
to hit the terrorists, these extremists,” an army
officer announced. An official, whose route home
has come under attack from rebels in Daraya,
said: “If I was afraid, I would just shut my door
and stay inside. I have to work and I am not
afraid. If I don’t defend my country, who will?”

Ordinary citizens, in private conversation, are
less defiant. In the centre of town, a shopkeeper
complained sadly that his baby daughter cries at
the sound of shelling. Zeina, a twenty-something
student, fears becoming desensitized to suffering
– and perhaps to danger too. “In the beginning,
when there started to be explosions, I used to
have nightmares,” she reflected. “Now I can sleep
through anything.”

And, the risks are multiplying even closer to
home. In Sabaa Bahrat Square, in what was
supposed to be the safest part of Damascus,
a car bomb detonated, leaving a blackened
concrete facade, broken windows and mangled
metal as well as blast damage to the imposing
structure of the Syrian Central Bank next door.

Mourning notices for two of the 15 victims –
Muhammad al-Sufi and Manal al-Tahan – are
stuck to the wall opposite. Scruffy, machine-gun
toting militiamen mill around the square, often
used for televised pro-regime rallies with civil
servants bussed in en masse to chant slogans
under giant banners of President Bashar
al-Assad.

That bombing was not the worst Damascus has
experienced as the situation has deteriorated. In
February, 80 people, including schoolchildren,
reportedly died near the ruling Ba’ath Party
headquarters in Mazraa. The crater is still visible,
marked by an enormous patch of fresh asphalt
on the main road going north. “I live nearby but
luckily I wasn’t there,” recalled Munir, a
university lecturer.

Mortar bombs, fired from rebel-held areas now
within easy range of the city, have become an
ominous novelty. The bombs killed 15 students
in a university cafeteria on 28 March. The
intended target is thought to have been a
government building.

Security measures have intensified since the
devastating bombing of the national security
crisis cell in July 2012, when four of Assad’s
most senior aides were killed. Concrete blast
barriers – often painted in the Syrian flag’s black,
red and white – now protect official premises, not
just the military or defence installations that are
obvious targets. The Iranian Embassy in Mezze,
its turquoise mosaic front giving an exotic glimpse
of Isfahan or Shiraz, looks like a fortress. “The
regime did manage to set up a ring of steel round
Damascus,” a foreign diplomat said. “But for
whatever reason the perimeter is starting to be
punctured and that brings home the reality of
the war.”

All this means that moving around has become
difficult, unpredictable and time-consuming –
another aspect of the new normal across an
understandably nervous city. Checkpoints on
main roads funnel traffic for ID checks and
baggage searches with handheld explosive
detectors – vital to stop future bombers. Only
drivers with an official security clearance can use
special fast lanes to avoid the wait.

It is hard, however, to avoid the question on
everyone’s mind: will there be a battle for
Damascus – the world’s oldest continually
inhabited city, as the guidebooks say – like the
one that has so damaged Aleppo? Parts of the
city already feel like a war zone: its ritziest hotel
is eerily deserted though many rooms are being
used as offices by international agencies drawn
by the deepening crisis – blue helmets and flak
jackets piled up on Persian carpets in an ornate
reception room, white UN vehicles parked behind
the blast barriers outside. The streets empty soon
after 9pm.

One view is that the fight for Syria’s capital
is coming, but not quite yet – in the summer
perhaps, some predict, when the rebels have
consolidated their gains in the south. Others
argue that outright victory by either side is
unlikely and hope for a political solution imposed
from abroad. But few here seem to expect things
to get any better.

versions.label: Version A
versions.text:
It is difficult to know exactly where the
noise is coming from, but you can hear
it everywhere in Damascus. All day and
all night you can hear the sound of guns,
rockets or planes attacking rebels – the
sound of war is getting closer to Syria’s
capital. The Syrian war began two years ago
and now the people of Damascus try not to
listen to the sound of explosions just a few
miles away.

“Actually you get used to it after a while,”
said George, who lives in the city. “But you
never know exactly what they are hitting.”
That usually becomes clear later from videos
that the opposition puts on YouTube.

The constant noise of bombs is more
worrying because the government tries so
hard to pretend that life is normal. “As you
can see, everything here is fine but we have
to hit the terrorists, these extremists,” an
army officer said. One government official
said: “If I was afraid, I would just shut my
door and stay inside. I have to work and I
am not afraid. If I don’t defend my country,
who will?”

In private conversation, ordinary people say
something different. In the centre of town, a
shopkeeper complained sadly that his baby
daughter cries at the sound of explosions.
Zeina, a student, worries that she has
learnt to live with suffering and danger. “In
the beginning, when there started to be
explosions, I used to have nightmares,” she
said. “Now I can sleep through anything.”

And, the dangers are increasing even closer
to home. Sabaa Bahrat Square was the
safest part of Damascus, but recently a car
bomb exploded there and damaged the
Syrian Central Bank. The square is often
used for pro-government rallies, with people
shouting slogans under enormous pictures
of President Bashar al-Assad.

That bombing was not the worst one in
Damascus in recent months. In February,
reports say that 80 people, including
schoolchildren, died near the ruling Ba’ath
Party headquarters in Mazraa. You can still
see the crater. “I live nearby but
luckily I wasn’t there,” says Munir, a
university lecturer.

Rebels, who are now very close to the city,
have recently started to fire mortar bombs.
The bombs killed 15 students in a university
cafeteria on 28 March. They probably
wanted to hit a government building.

In July 2012, a bomb killed four of Assad’s
senior aides. After that, security increased.
Concrete barriers – often painted in the
Syrian flag’s black, red and white – now
protect official buildings, not just the military
or defence installations that are
obvious targets.

Moving around the city has become difficult
and takes a lot of time – another part of life
today in a nervous city. Checkpoints on main
roads stop traffic for ID checks and bags are
searched for explosives. Only drivers with
official permission can use special fast lanes
to avoid the wait.

There is one question on everyone’s mind:
will there be a battle for Damascus – one of
the world’s oldest cities – like the one that
has badly damaged Aleppo? One view is
that there will be a battle for Syria’s capital,
but not yet – in the summer perhaps. Others
argue that there will probably not be a
complete victory for either side and hope for
a political solution that comes from abroad.
But most people here do not expect things
to get better.

versions.label: Version B
versions.text:
It is hard to know exactly where the noise comes from, but everyone in Damascus can hear it. Day and night, you hear the dull thuds and booms of artillery, rockets, or planes hitting rebel positions. The war is getting closer to Syria’s capital. But a little more than two years into the Syrian crisis—the longest and bloodiest of the Arab uprisings—many people in Damascus have started to ignore the sound of death and destruction. This has become the new normal.

Over the weekend, men smoked water pipes in a park with palm trees. Children played near the flowers. Couples talked on benches. At the same time, a loud blast could be heard a few miles away. Smoke rose between the minarets of a nearby Ottoman-era mosque. No one seemed to notice.

"Actually you do get used to it after a while," said George, an IT technician from a village on the coast. "But you never know exactly what they are hitting." Later, people often learn from video clips posted by opposition media outlets on YouTube.

The background noise is even more upsetting because the government tries hard to act like life is normal. "As you can see, everything here is fine but we have to hit the terrorists, these extremists," an army officer said. An official said his way home has been attacked by rebels in Daraya. He said, "If I was afraid, I would just shut my door and stay inside. I have to work and I am not afraid. If I don’t defend my country, who will?"

In private, ordinary citizens speak differently. In the center of town, a shopkeeper said sadly that his baby daughter cries when she hears shelling. Zeina, a student in her twenties, is afraid she is getting used to suffering—and maybe to danger too. "In the beginning, when there started to be explosions, I used to have nightmares," she said. "Now I can sleep through anything."

The danger is also getting closer to home. In Sabaa Bahrat Square, which was supposed to be the safest part of Damascus, a car bomb exploded. It left a blackened concrete front, broken windows, twisted metal, and damage to the Syrian Central Bank next door.

Notices of mourning for two of the 15 victims—Muhammad al-Sufi and Manal al-Tahan—are on a wall across the street. Scruffy militiamen with machine guns walk around the square. The square is often used for televised pro-regime rallies, with civil servants brought in large numbers to chant slogans under huge banners of President Bashar al-Assad.

That bombing was not the worst in Damascus as the situation has gotten worse. In February, 80 people, including schoolchildren, reportedly died near the ruling Ba’ath Party headquarters in Mazraa. The crater is still there. It is marked by a very large patch of new asphalt on the main road going north. "I live nearby but luckily I wasn’t there," said Munir, a university lecturer.

Mortar bombs, fired from rebel-held areas that are now close enough to reach the city, have become a scary new thing. These bombs killed 15 students in a university cafeteria on 28 March. The target was thought to be a government building.

Security has become tighter since the very bad bombing of the national security crisis cell in July 2012, when four of Assad’s most senior aides were killed. Concrete blast barriers, often painted in the Syrian flag’s black, red, and white, now protect many official buildings, not only military or defense places. The Iranian Embassy in Mezze, with a turquoise mosaic front that looks like a glimpse of Isfahan or Shiraz, now looks like a fortress. "The regime did manage to set up a ring of steel round Damascus," a foreign diplomat said. "But for whatever reason the perimeter is starting to be punctured and that brings home the reality of the war."

Because of all this, moving around the city is difficult, unpredictable, and slow. This is another part of the new normal in a nervous city. Checkpoints on main roads force cars into lines for ID checks and bag searches. Soldiers use handheld explosive detectors to try to stop future bombers. Only drivers with an official security clearance can use special fast lanes to avoid the long wait.

Many people keep thinking about one question: will there be a battle for Damascus—the world’s oldest continually inhabited city, as guidebooks say—like the battle that has badly damaged Aleppo? Some parts of the city already feel like a war zone. The richest hotel feels empty, although many rooms are used as offices by international agencies because the crisis is getting worse. Blue helmets and flak jackets are piled on Persian carpets in a decorated reception room. White UN vehicles are parked behind blast barriers outside. The streets become empty soon after 9pm.

Some people think the fight for Syria’s capital is coming, but not yet—maybe in the summer, some predict, when the rebels have strengthened their gains in the south. Others say neither side is likely to win clearly and they hope for a political solution forced from abroad. But few people here expect things to get better.

versions.label: Version C
versions.text:
It is hard to know exactly where the noise comes from, but everyone in Damascus can hear it. Day and night there are dull booms from artillery, rockets, or planes hitting rebel areas. The war is coming closer to Syria’s capital. More than two years into the Syrian crisis, many people in Damascus have gotten used to hearing death and destruction nearby.

Over the weekend, men smoked water pipes in a park with palm trees. Children played near flowerbeds, and couples talked on benches. Loud explosions could be heard a few miles away. Smoke rose near the minarets of an old Ottoman-era mosque. No one seemed to notice.

“Actually you do get used to it after a while,” said George, an IT technician from a village on the coast. “But you never know exactly what they are hitting.” People often learn later from videos posted by opposition media outlets on YouTube.

The sounds are even more upsetting because the government tries to act like life is normal. An army officer said, “As you can see, everything here is fine but we have to hit the terrorists, these extremists.” An official, whose way home has been attacked by rebels in Daraya, said, “If I was afraid, I would just shut my door and stay inside. I have to work and I am not afraid. If I don’t defend my country, who will?”

In private, ordinary people speak differently. A shopkeeper in the center of town said sadly that his baby daughter cries when she hears shelling. Zeina, a student in her twenties, worries she is getting used to suffering, and maybe to danger too. “In the beginning, when there started to be explosions, I used to have nightmares,” she said. “Now I can sleep through anything.”

The danger is also getting closer to home. In Sabaa Bahrat Square, in what was supposed to be the safest part of Damascus, a car bomb exploded. It left blackened concrete, broken windows, twisted metal, and damage to the Syrian Central Bank building next door.

Notices of mourning for two of the 15 victims, Muhammad al-Sufi and Manal al-Tahan, are stuck to a wall nearby. Untidy militiamen with machine guns stand around the square. The square is often used for televised pro-regime rallies, where civil servants are brought in large groups to chant slogans under huge banners of President Bashar al-Assad.

That bombing was not the worst Damascus has seen as things have gotten worse. In February, 80 people, including schoolchildren, reportedly died near the ruling Ba’ath Party headquarters in Mazraa. The crater can still be seen, marked by a large patch of new asphalt on the main road going north. “I live nearby but luckily I wasn’t there,” said Munir, a university lecturer.

Mortar bombs, fired from rebel-held areas now close enough to reach the city easily, have become a frightening new danger. These bombs killed 15 students in a university cafeteria on 28 March. The target was thought to be a government building.

Security has become stronger since the bombing of the national security crisis cell in July 2012, when four of Assad’s most senior aides were killed. Concrete blast barriers, often painted black, red, and white like the Syrian flag, now protect many official places, not only military sites. The Iranian Embassy in Mezze, with a turquoise mosaic front, now looks like a fortress. A foreign diplomat said, “The regime did manage to set up a ring of steel round Damascus. But for whatever reason the perimeter is starting to be punctured and that brings home the reality of the war.”

Because of this, moving around the city is hard, uncertain, and slow. Checkpoints on main roads make traffic stop for ID checks and bag searches. Soldiers use handheld explosive detectors to try to stop future bombers. Only drivers with official security clearance can use special fast lanes to avoid waiting.

Many people are thinking about one big question: will there be a battle for Damascus, like the one that has badly damaged Aleppo? Parts of Damascus already feel like a war zone. The most expensive hotel is strangely empty, even though international agencies use many rooms as offices because of the growing crisis. Blue helmets and flak jackets are piled on Persian carpets in a decorated reception room. White UN vehicles are parked behind blast barriers outside. The streets become empty soon after 9pm.

Some people think the fight for the capital is coming, but not yet, maybe in the summer, after rebels strengthen their gains in the south. Others think neither side will win completely and hope for a political solution pushed from abroad. But few people in Damascus expect things to get better soon.

study_items.title: Nigerian Low Tech
study_items.original_text:
When it comes to climate change, we havethe bad habit of focusing on the  rst part ofthe story, the part about the problem, and forgetting the second part about the many available solutions. These solutions are speeding up recycling, slowing down emissions and providing sustainable alternatives to plastic, air conditioning, smartphones and fast fashion.

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change recently gathered in Copenhagen to present its latest report on the impacts and pace of climate change. Climate change is now measured onall continents and our efforts to lower emissions must be intensi ed to avoid it escalating outof control. Along with outlining the risks and challenges, Copenhagen also embraced and focused on the solutions.

In the spirit of focusing on what can bedone, Sustainia Award, chaired by Arnold Schwarzenegger, celebrated ten leading sustainability solutions deployed in 84 countries. From food to fashion, energy to transportation, education to health, the awards showcased an alternative to the grim-future scenarios we are so often presented with and made sustainability tangible to the innovators, investors, consumers and policy makers across sectors and regions.

From California, we saw how we can now produce plastics from greenhouse gases that are competitive with normal oil-based plastics in price and quality. From Switzerland, we learned how we can recycle and reuse old clothes and shoes more effectively in a recycle system currently deployed in over 60 countries. And from Canada, we learned how smartphones can make bike- sharing more convenient.

The ten projects presented each offered unique solutions to sustainability challenges but it was the Nigerian initiative, Wecyclers, that won Arnold SchwarzeneggerÕs, and the rest of juryÕs, vote and took the Sustainia Award 2014.

Wecyclers enables low-income communitiesto make money on waste piling up in theirstreets. By deploying a  eet of cargo bicycles to collect and recycle unmanaged waste in Lagos, Wecyclers lets families exchange garbage for consumer goods via an SMS-based point system.

Recycling companies purchase WecyclersÕ sorted waste for reprocessing into products Ð theyturn it into mattresses, pillows and trash bags. Wecyclers is a response to local waste issues, where itÕs estimated that only 40% of the cityÕs rubbish is collected. According to the World Bank, only 46% of municipal solid waste in Africa is collected. More than 5,000 households have signed up so far and there are plans to extend the initiative to other cities throughout Nigeria.

Solutions to combat climate change are often perceived as hi-tech innovations focused on cutting emissions, creating infrastructure or ef ciency. However, to successfully solve the variety of challenges, we need variety in our solutions as well. Sustainability is not solely a matter of bringing down emissions; it is also a question of using our natural resources more intelligently and creating healthier lives for ourselves. Initiatives might be low-tech in innovation but high-impact when itcomes to creating sustainable change for entire communities.

With a wide range of solutions addressing the equally wide range of challenges, we must focus more on the important part of the story that creates enthusiasm and momentum, and spurs action for much-needed change.

versions.label: Version A
versions.text:
When we talk about climate change, we often focus only on the problem and forget that there are many solutions. Solutions can help us recycle faster, reduce emissions, and find greener choices instead of plastic, air conditioning, smartphones, and fast fashion.

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change met in Copenhagen to share its newest report. Climate change is now measured on all continents. We must work harder to lower emissions so the problem does not get out of control. The meeting also talked about solutions, not only risks.

Sustainia Award, led by Arnold Schwarzenegger, honored ten strong sustainability solutions used in 84 countries. The solutions covered many areas, like food, fashion, energy, transportation, education, and health. The awards showed that a better future is possible and helped innovators, investors, consumers, and policy makers.

From California, people showed a way to make plastic from greenhouse gases. This plastic can match oil-based plastic in price and quality. From Switzerland, people showed how to recycle and reuse old clothes and shoes better, using a system that is in over 60 countries. From Canada, people showed how smartphones can make bike sharing easier.

All ten projects had different ideas, but the Nigerian project Wecyclers won the Sustainia Award 2014.

Wecyclers helps low-income communities earn money from waste in their streets. In Lagos, it uses cargo bicycles to collect and recycle waste. Families can trade their garbage for consumer goods using an SMS point system.

Recycling companies buy Wecyclers’ sorted waste and turn it into new products, like mattresses, pillows, and trash bags. Wecyclers answers a local problem, because only about 40% of the city’s rubbish is collected. The World Bank says only 46% of municipal solid waste in Africa is collected. More than 5,000 households have joined so far, and there are plans to expand to other cities in Nigeria.

People often think climate change solutions must be high-tech and focused only on cutting emissions, building infrastructure, or improving efficiency. But we need many kinds of solutions. Sustainability is not only about lowering emissions. It is also about using natural resources wisely and creating healthier lives. Some ideas may be low-tech but can still make a big difference for whole communities.

Because there are many challenges and many solutions, we should focus more on the part of the story that gives hope, builds energy, and leads to action for needed change.

versions.label: Version B
versions.text:
When we talk about climate change, we usually just talk about the problem. We usually forget the many solutions. These solutions make recycling faster, reduce emissions and create alternatives to plastic, air conditioning, smartphones and fast fashion that are better for the environment.

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change met in Copenhagen to present its latest report. There is now climate change on all continents. We must increase our efforts to reduce emissions to make sure that climate change does not get out of control. Copenhagen looked at the risks and challenges but it also looked at the solutions.

The Sustainia Award looked at ten best solutions to climate change. The solutions are used in84 countries. From the areas of food, fashion, energy, transport, education and health, the awards showed that the future may not beso bad.

From California, we saw how we can now produce plastics from greenhouse gases. These plastics are good quality and not too expensive. From Switzerland, we learned a better way to recycle and reuse old clothes and shoes. And from Canada, we learned how smartphones can make bike-sharing easier.

The ten projects each offered unique solutions to the problem of climate change. The Nigerian project, Wecyclers, won the SustainiaAward 2014.

Wecyclers makes it possible for poor communities to make money from the waste from their streets. Families in Lagos collect the rubbish on the streets. Then, bicycles come and collect the waste. Families get points for the garbage they collect. They can use these points to get things they need.

Recycling companies buy WecyclersÕ waste. They make the waste into products such as mattresses, pillows and trash bags. Wecyclers helps to solve local waste problems in Lagos, where only 40% of the cityÕs rubbish is collected.

Only 46% of town and city waste in Africa is collected. More than 5,000 families are involved in the Wecyclers scheme and there are plans to start the project in other cities in Nigeria.

Solutions to climate change are often hi-tech. But, to solve all the different problems, weneed different solutions. We canÕt just reduce emissions Ð we must also use our natural resources more intelligently and create healthier lives for ourselves.

versions.label: Version C
versions.text:
With climate change, we often focus on the first part of the story: the problem. We forget the second part: many solutions are ready now. These solutions include faster recycling, lower emissions, and sustainable options instead of plastic, air conditioning, smartphones, and fast fashion.

The Intergovernmental Panel on Climate Change recently met in Copenhagen to present its latest report about the effects and speed of climate change. Climate change is now measured on all continents. Our efforts to lower emissions must be stronger so it does not grow out of control. The meeting in Copenhagen also focused on solutions, not only on risks and problems.

To focus on what can be done, the Sustainia Award, chaired by Arnold Schwarzenegger, celebrated ten leading sustainability solutions used in 84 countries. The awards covered many areas, like food, fashion, energy, transportation, education, and health. They showed real alternatives to the dark future stories we often hear. They made sustainability feel real for innovators, investors, consumers, and policy makers.

From California, we saw that we can now make plastics from greenhouse gases. These plastics can compete with normal oil-based plastics in price and quality. From Switzerland, we learned how to recycle and reuse old clothes and shoes better, in a recycling system used in over 60 countries. From Canada, we learned how smartphones can make bike-sharing easier.

The ten projects each had different solutions, but the Nigerian project, Wecyclers, won Arnold Schwarzenegger's and the rest of the jury's vote. It won the Sustainia Award 2014.

Wecyclers helps low-income communities earn money from waste that piles up in their streets. It uses a fleet of cargo bicycles to collect and recycle unmanaged waste in Lagos. Families can trade garbage for consumer goods using an SMS-based point system.

Recycling companies buy Wecyclers' sorted waste and process it into products. They turn it into mattresses, pillows, and trash bags. Wecyclers answers local waste problems. It is estimated that only 40% of the city's rubbish is collected. According to the World Bank, only 46% of municipal solid waste in Africa is collected. More than 5,000 households have signed up so far, and there are plans to expand to other cities in Nigeria.

People often think climate solutions must be hi-tech and focused on cutting emissions, building infrastructure, or improving efficiency. But to solve many different problems, we also need many different kinds of solutions. Sustainability is not only about lowering emissions. It is also about using natural resources in a smarter way and creating healthier lives. Some projects may be low-tech, but they can still create big, lasting change for whole communities.

Because there are many challenges and many solutions, we should focus more on the part of the story that builds excitement, creates momentum, and pushes people to act for needed change.

study_items.title: Ten Ideas
study_items.original_text:
From glow-in-the-dark trees to underground bike sheds and solar-powered bins, we look at some of the more left eld solutions to help make our cities more livable.City living has many upsides but a sustainable lifestyle is increasingly not among them. Pollution, traf c and loss of green spaces are just some of the daily trials that city-dwellers have to deal with.Step forward the inventors. We consider ten of the wackier solutions to making our cities more livable.

Pop-up parksTodayÕs cities sometimes look like theyÕre built more for cars than people. The pop-up park is a simple idea. Take an empty car park, a pocketful of change and a pot plant or two, and make yourself your own private park. The PARK(ing) project kicked off asan arts experiment in San Francisco and has since spread across the world. Temporary urban farms and ecology demos are just some of the ideas to have emerged out of the movement, which celebrates a day of action every September.

Subterranean storageNot all urban dwellers are hooked to the car. Bikes are ever more in vogue. The question is: where to keep it safe? Tokyo-based engineering  rm Giken has come up with a concept for a solution: an Òeco- cycle, anti-seismic underground bicycle parkÓ. At just seven metres wide, the cylindrical storage facility buries deep enough into the ground to house 204 bikes. Owners can retrieve their bike at the touchof a button, with the automated system delivering it back above ground in around 13 seconds.

Glow-in-the-dark treesWhen most people think of trees that glow in the dark, Christmas baubles and fallen pine needles usually come to mind. Not Daan Roosegaarde.The Dutch designer-artist has comes up with a ÒbioluminescentÓ plant. The experimental technology splices DNA from luminescent marine bacteriawith the chloroplast genome of a plant to create a jelly sh-type glow. Trials are underway to create an industrial-scale version of the biomimicry-inspired technology that Roosegaarde hopes could one day replace conventional street lighting.

Footfall harvestingEvery day, hundreds of commuters and shoppers in the east London neighbourhood of West Ham cross the elevated pedestrian walkway close to the underground station. Few probably notice the springiness beneath their feet. Fewer still connect that  ve-millimetre  ex in the rubber surface tothe powering of the streetlights above. The paved  ooring is decked with smart tiles that capturethe kinetic energy from pedestriansÕ footstepsand convert it into electricity. Pavegen, the UK rm behind the innovation, has installed a similar system at LondonÕs Heathrow Airport, among other international locations.

SupertreesIt had to happen eventually: man-made trees. SingaporeÕs Gardens by the Bay has a small copse of them. Up to 50 metres high, thesesteel-framed ÔsupertreesÕ not only have  owers and ferns growing up them but their metallic canopies act to absorb and disperse heat too. TheyÕre equipped to harvest rainwater, too, as well as provide air ventilation for two Òclimate-controlled biomesÓ(large conservatories, in other words) below.Eleven of the 18 trees also boast solar panels along their ÔbranchesÕ.

versions.label: Version A
versions.text:
From glow-in-the-dark trees to bike storage under the ground and solar-powered bins, people are trying unusual ideas to make cities better places to live.

Living in a city can be good, but it is often hard to live in a sustainable way. City people deal with pollution, traffic, and less green space. So inventors are trying new ideas. Here are some of the wackier solutions.

Pop-up parks
Some cities look like they are built more for cars than for people. A pop-up park is a simple idea. You take an empty car park, some coins, and one or two plants, and you make a small park for a short time. The PARK(ing) project started as an art experiment in San Francisco and then spread around the world. People have also made temporary city farms and ecology demos. The movement has a day of action every September.

Subterranean storage
Not everyone in cities uses a car. Bikes are becoming more popular. But where can you keep a bike safe? A Tokyo-based engineering firm called Giken made an idea called an “eco-cycle, anti-seismic underground bicycle park.” It is a round storage space that is only seven metres wide, and it goes down into the ground. It can store 204 bikes. Owners can get their bike back by pressing a button. The automatic system brings it up in about 13 seconds.

Glow-in-the-dark trees
When many people think of trees that glow, they think of Christmas lights. But Dutch designer-artist Daan Roosegaarde made a “bioluminescent” plant. This experimental technology mixes DNA from glowing marine bacteria with the chloroplast genome of a plant. This makes a glow like a jellyfish. Trials are happening to make a large-scale version. Roosegaarde hopes it could one day replace normal street lights.

Footfall harvesting
In West Ham in east London, many commuters and shoppers walk across an elevated walkway near the underground station. Most people do not notice that the ground feels a little springy. The rubber surface bends about five millimetres. This helps power the streetlights above. The floor has smart tiles that capture energy from footsteps and turn it into electricity. The UK firm Pavegen made this idea. Pavegen has also installed a similar system at London’s Heathrow Airport and other places.

Supertrees
Singapore’s Gardens by the Bay has man-made trees called “supertrees.” They are steel-framed and can be up to 50 metres high. Flowers and ferns grow on them. Their metal tops can absorb and spread heat. They can collect rainwater and help provide air ventilation for two “climate-controlled biomes” below (large conservatories). Eleven of the 18 trees also have solar panels on their “branches.”

versions.label: Version B
versions.text:
From glow-in-the-dark trees to bike sheds under the ground and bins that use solar power, here are some unusual ideas to make cities better places to live.

Living in a city has good points, but it is harder to live in a sustainable way. People deal with pollution, traffic, and fewer green spaces. Inventors have ideas to help. Here are ten of the strangest ideas.

Pop-up parks
Some cities seem built more for cars than for people. A pop-up park is a simple idea. You take an empty car park, a little money, and one or two plants, and you make a small park. The PARK(ing) project started as an art experiment in San Francisco. It has now spread around the world. People also make temporary city farms and ecology demonstrations. The movement has a day of action every September.

Underground storage
Not everyone in cities uses a car. Bikes are getting more popular. But where can you keep a bike safe? An engineering company in Tokyo called Giken made an idea called an “eco-cycle, anti-seismic underground bicycle park”. It is a round storage place that is only seven metres wide. It goes deep into the ground and can hold 204 bikes. The owner can get the bike back by pressing a button. The system brings it up in about 13 seconds.

Glow-in-the-dark trees
Many people think of Christmas lights when they think about things that glow at night. But Daan Roosegaarde thinks about glowing plants. This Dutch designer and artist created an experimental “bioluminescent” plant. The technology mixes DNA from glowing sea bacteria with part of a plant’s genes. This makes a glow like a jellyfish. Trials are happening to make an industrial-scale version. Roosegaarde hopes it could one day replace normal street lights.

Footstep power
Every day, many commuters and shoppers in West Ham in east London cross a raised walkway near the underground station. Most people do not notice that the ground feels a little springy. Even fewer know this helps power the streetlights above. The floor has smart tiles. The tiles catch energy from people’s footsteps and turn it into electricity. The UK company Pavegen made this system. It has also put a similar system in London’s Heathrow Airport and in other places around the world.

Supertrees
People have made man-made trees. Singapore’s Gardens by the Bay has a small group of them. These steel “supertrees” can be up to 50 metres tall. They have flowers and ferns growing on them. Their metal tops also help take in and spread heat. They can collect rainwater and help bring air to two “climate-controlled biomes” (large conservatories) below. Eleven of the 18 trees also have solar panels on their “branches”.

versions.label: Version C
versions.text:
There are many quirky solutions to help make our cities better places to live, such as glow-in-the-dark trees, underground bike sheds and solar-powered bins.City living is good in many ways but it is not always possible to lead a sustainable lifestyle in a city. Pollution, traf c and loss of green spaces are just some of the daily problems that people in cities have to live with.We look at ten quirky solutions that could make our cities better places to live.

Pop-up parksTodayÕs cities sometimes look like theyÕre built for cars and not for people. The pop-up park is a simple idea. You need an empty car park, a small amount of money and a plant or two, and you can make your own private park. The PARK(ing) project started as an arts experiment in San Francisco. It has since spread across the world.

Underground storageNot everyone in a city has a car. Bikes are in fashion but there is one problem: where to keep the bike safe? An engineering company in Tokyo has a solution: an underground bicycle park. Just seven metres wide, the bicycle park goes deep enough into the ground to keep 204 bikes.

Glow-in-the-dark treesWhen most people think of trees that glow in the dark, they usually think of Christmas trees. Not Daan Roosegaarde. The Dutch designer-artist has invented a plant that is like a light. The technology joins DNA from glowing marine bacteria with a plant to create a glow like a jelly sh. Roosegaarde hopes that this technology could one day replace normal street lighting.

Pedestrian electricityEvery day, hundreds of people in the east London neighbourhood of West Ham cross a pedestrian walkway close to the underground station. They probably donÕt notice the springiness of the walkway. And they probably donÕt know that the springy rubber surface powers the streetlights above. The  oorhas tiles that capture the energy from pedestriansÕ footsteps and turn it into electricity. There is a similar system at LondonÕs Heathrow Airport.

SupertreesSingaporeÕs Gardens by the Bay has a group of man-made trees. Up to 50 metres high, these steel ÔsupertreesÕ have  owers growing up them. They collect rainwater and 11 of the 18 trees also have solar panels on their ÔbranchesÕ.
